intro:
Cinnamon was a highly prized aromatic spice in the ancient world, derived from the inner bark of the Cinnamomum verum tree native to Sri Lanka and southern India. In Scripture it appears as a component of the holy anointing oil prescribed in Exodus 30:23, indicating its sacred and costly character. It also features in the erotic poetry of Song of Solomon 4:14 among the choicest perfumes, and in Proverbs 7:17 as a scent associated with seduction and luxury.

In the New Testament, cinnamon appears in Revelation 18:13 among the luxury goods traded in Babylon, whose loss the merchants of the earth lament. This placement signals cinnamon's status as a premium trade commodity throughout the ancient Mediterranean, transported overland and by sea from distant eastern sources. Its consistent association in Scripture with anointing, worship, and excess underscores its dual role as both a sacred ingredient and a symbol of worldly indulgence.
